The research tools returned no relevant amyloid-kinetics literature (the corpus is ML-focused and web search failed). I'll note this and proceed from established first-principles biophysics that I already know well.

**Research note / justification from domain knowledge (Cohen, Knowles, Michaels et al. amyloid master equation, PNAS 2013 / Nat. Chem. 2018):** Aβ42 aggregation is *secondary-nucleation dominated*. The key insight for inhibitor structure is that most effective inhibitors (e.g., Brichos chaperone, small molecules) act by **suppressing the surface-catalyzed secondary nucleation feedback** — the autocatalytic term where existing fibril mass templates new nuclei. Critically, this shifts the **lag time / half-time** while leaving the plateau intact, because the inhibitor slows the *amplification* of new nuclei rather than the final monomer depletion. state²`: this quadratic surface-catalyzed term produces the sharp sigmoidal ignition characteristic of Aβ42 and is where the inhibitor delay bites hardest, sharpening both NMSE and shape fit across cd values.
